mission.title: Install and Configure Kured on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kured (Kubernetes Reboot Daemon) is a Kubernetes daemonset that automatically reboots nodes when necessary, ensuring safe operations by managing node cordoning and draining. Installing Kured helps maintain the health of your Kubernetes cluster by handling reboots seamlessly.

steps.title: Add the Kured Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the Kured Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration:
```bash
helm repo add kured https://kubereboot.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kured using Helm
steps.description:
Next, install Kured in your Kubernetes cluster using the following command. Replace `<namespace>` with your desired namespace (e.g., `kured`):
```bash
helm install kured kured/kured --namespace <namespace> --create-namespace --version 1.21.0
```

steps.title: Verify Kured installation
steps.description:
To ensure Kured is running correctly, check the status of the pods in the specified namespace:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n <namespace>
```

steps.title: Configure Kured for resource limits
steps.description:
You may want to set resource limits for Kured. Edit the Helm release with the following command:
```bash
helm upgrade kured kured/kured --namespace <namespace> --set resources.limits.cpu=100m --set resources.limits.memory=128Mi
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for Kured
steps.description:
To ensure Kured has the necessary permissions, apply the following RBAC configuration:
```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: kured
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "patch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["kubelet.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["create", "delete"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: kured
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: kured
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: default
  namespace: <namespace>
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kured will show the Kured pods running in the specified namespace, and you will have configured resource limits and RBAC permissions. Kured will now automatically manage node reboots as needed.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kured https://kubereboot.github.io/charts
helm repo update
helm upgrade kured kured/kured --namespace <namespace> --set resources.limits.cpu=100m --set resources.limits.memory=128Mi

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: kured
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "patch"]
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["kubelet.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["create", "delete"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: kured
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: kured
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: default
  namespace: <namespace>

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To uninstall Kured, first remove the Helm release using the following command. Replace `<namespace>` with your specified namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall kured --namespace <namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Next, delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources that were created during the installation:
```bash
kubectl delete crd kureds.kured.dev
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, if you no longer need the namespace, delete it and verify that all resources have been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace <namespace>
kubectl get all -n <namespace>
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current Helm release configuration. You can do this by exporting the current values:
```bash
helm get values kured --namespace <namespace> > kured-backup-values.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and then upgrade Kured to the latest version. Replace `<namespace>` with your specified namespace:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kured kured/kured --namespace <namespace> --version 1.22.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check the status of the pods to ensure that Kured is running the new version correctly:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice that Kured pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, you can diagnose the issue by checking the logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <namespace>
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Kured not draining nodes
troubleshooting.description:
If Kured is not draining nodes as expected, check the Kured logs for any errors related to node cordoning:
```bash
kubectl logs <kured-pod-name> -n <namespace>
```
Ensure that the RBAC permissions are correctly set and that Kured has access to manage nodes.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during the Helm upgrade, check the currently installed version and the version you are trying to upgrade to:
```bash
helm list --namespace <namespace>
```
Make sure you are using a compatible version and try upgrading again.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing pod eviction
troubleshooting.description:
If Kured pods are being evicted due to resource limits, check the events in the namespace:
```bash
kubectl get events -n <namespace>
```
Consider adjusting the resource limits set during installation to provide more resources to the Kured pods.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
